title: Melon Makes It Two in a Row as Kaka Stuns on Debut and Aizawa's Heart Breaks
meta: Published: 26 April 2026 | By Samira Al-Rashid, Emerging Talent Scout | F1CC News
caption: Ryan Melon celebrates his second consecutive F2 feature race victory, with debutant Ricardo Kaka and Alex Thompson joining him on the podium.
body:
Ryan Melon is making this look easy. Two feature races, two victories. The Rodin Motorsport driver followed up his Bahrain triumph with a controlled, commanding win in the Jeddah feature race, extending his championship lead to 15 points and cementing his status as the man to beat. But if the winner was familiar, the podium was anything but. Next to Melon stood Ricardo Kaka, a guest driver on his F2 debut, who took a stunning second place. And alongside them, Alex Thompson — the Mercedes junior who has battled through self-doubt and a split schedule — claimed his best finish of the season in third. Mark my words: this championship is being shaped by the drivers who want it most, and right now Ryan Melon wants it more than anyone.

The Jeddah Corniche Circuit is a unforgiving place — high-speed, lined with walls, and punishing of the smallest error. Feature qualifying had already thrown up shocks, with sprint winner Korv Batt knocked out in Q1 and Corentin Premier, fastest in Q1, eliminated in Q2. The pole shootout belonged to Takumi Aizawa, the Campos Racing driver with a reputation for raw pace but a career interrupted by budget shortfalls and a season spent scrapping for a seat. He delivered a lap of 1:29.670 — his first F2 pole — and lined up ahead of Jacob Vavro, Thompson, Andrew Price, and Charles Mounir. Championship leader Melon started sixth, a position that would prove crucial as the race unfolded.

At lights out, Aizawa held the lead, but behind him the midfield was already churning. Daniel Park locked up at Turn 1 and dropped to the back, while Vavro and Thompson gave chase. By lap 10, Aizawa had built a lead of over three seconds, and the pit window began to shuffle the order. The leaders cycled through their stops, and by lap 21 Aizawa was back in front — a pole-sitter controlling his race, a maiden victory in sight. Then, cruelly, the car began to fail. A mechanical issue — later confirmed as a drivetrain problem — first slowed him, then stopped him entirely. He pulled off the circuit, his helmet in his hands, a victory stolen by the machinery beneath him. For a driver who has fought so hard to reach this point, the silence in the Campos garage was heavier than any noise could be.

The retirement handed the lead to Yann Asdelian briefly, but the DAMS driver had not yet stopped and soon pitted, leaving the door open for Melon. The Rodin driver, who had been quietly working his way forward, seized the opportunity and never looked back. His pace was relentless, his tyre management flawless, and by the time the chequered flag fell, he was 16 seconds up the road. Two feature races, two wins — and a championship lead that is starting to look imposing.

Behind him, the story of the race belonged to Ricardo Kaka. The guest driver, brought in by Rodin Motorsport for this weekend, had never started an F2 race before Friday. He qualified eighth, finished seventh in the sprint, and then delivered a feature race performance that stunned the paddock. His second place was built on intelligent pit timing, clean overtakes, and a composure that belied his inexperience. On a circuit that punishes hesitation, Kaka never put a wheel wrong. He crossed the line 16 seconds behind Melon but five seconds clear of Alex Thompson in third, and the Rodin garage celebrated a one-two that felt like a statement. Kaka's future is uncertain — he is not a full-time F2 driver — but his performance will not be forgotten.

Alex Thompson's third place was another chapter in a season of quiet resurgence. The Mercedes junior, who splits his F2 campaign with a GT3 Spring Challenge programme, has faced more challenges than most. His post-race radio in Bahrain captured the emotion of a driver who thought his season was over. Jeddah proved it is very much alive. Third place, a best finish of the year, and a podium that moves him to fifth in the championship. He spoke afterwards about the team's progress and his own growing confidence, his voice steady but the smile impossible to hide.

The midfield delivered its usual chaos. Hamish Porter drove from tenth on the grid to fourth, a result that keeps him second in the standings and underlines his relentless consistency. Kodiak Callahan, the Australian DAMS driver, charged from 19th to fifth — an 14-place gain — and was voted Driver of the Day. His overtakes were brave, his pace on the medium compound exceptional, and his result a reminder that he is still very much in the championship fight. Frederik Frid took sixth for Van Amersfoort Racing, Corentin Premier recovered from his Q2 exit to finish seventh, and Jaromir Grunt salvaged eighth for AIX Racing after a difficult weekend.

Further back, the heartbreak was real. Takumi Aizawa's mechanical retirement was the cruelest blow, but he was not alone. Marcos Olmedo also stopped with a mechanical issue, leaving Campos Racing with a double DNF and a paddock full of sympathy. Marek Dubišar, the Invicta driver who had been a title contender after Bahrain, suffered a puncture late in the race. He limped back to the pits, changed tyres, and continued — finishing last, a lap down, his championship hopes damaged but his spirit intact. In F2, the margins are fine, and a single puncture can change a season.

The championship picture now has a familiar shape. Melon leads with 56 points. Porter is second on 41. Callahan's stunning weekend vaults him to third on 34, with Grunt fourth on 33 and Thompson fifth on 31. The top five are separated by just 25 points. Dubišar, who led the championship after Bahrain, slips to sixth on 26. In the constructors' fight, Rodin Motorsport's one-two has propelled them to the top with 76 points, 9 clear of Invicta Racing. DAMS sit third with 52, Van Amersfoort Racing fourth with 45, and Trident — buoyed by Thompson's podium — are fifth with 39. AIX Racing, the defending champions, are sixth with 37.

The paddock now heads back to Europe, but the memories of Jeddah will linger. A pole-sitter denied by his car. A debutant who came from nowhere. A champion who refuses to be beaten. And a grid of young drivers, all of them hungry, all of them believing they can be the one. Mark my words: this is the year Formula 2 proves that the future of motorsport is already here. The only question is who will be standing at the top when it arrives.
